title: Object-Format Transition, Compatibility, and Disaster Defense · Independent Repository Trial
summary: An independent maintainer receives a different graph, repository policy, and failure constraint and must transfer object-format transition, compatibility, and disaster defense into a new Git 3.0 readiness and disaster-recovery defense. Planned artifact: Produce an independent changed-context version of Git 3.0 readiness and disaster-recovery defense with automated and judgment evidence. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence…
objectives: Explain SHA-1 and SHA-256 repository object formats, repository initialization choice, ID length, storage identity, and why algorithms are not a cosmetic display option.
Evaluate compatibility object formats, translation tables, protocol exchange, clone and fetch interoperability, tooling assumptions, and repository immutability limits.
Inventory scripts, parsers, databases, APIs, hooks, CI, hosting, and documentation for Git 3.0 breaking defaults, long IDs, and version-gated behavior.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# git-advanced cumulative repository evidence notebook
# Git operations change only disposable simulated repository state.
# Inspect status, diffs, graph, refs, and recovery before every mutation.

steps.title: Commit to the mission model
steps.instruction:
lab case — Case G28 is a lab in a disposable repository. the repository stores SHA-256 objects while one integration still assumes 40-character IDs. The learner must predict format evidence separates storage identity from accepted input and displayed output formats, run git rev-parse --show-object-format=storage,input,output, explain storage reports sha256 and the audit parser fails its fixed-width object-ID contract, test this changed condition: restore a bundle into a fresh same-format repository and reject an incompatible toolchain, and preserve this recovery boundary: preserve trusted refs, object stores, translation data, manifests, and exact loss accounting.

What must be established before implementation?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for explain sha-1 and sha-256 repository object formats, repository initialization choice, id length, storage identity, and why algorithms are not a cosmetic display option.

steps.title: Extend the cumulative artifact
steps.instruction: Append an Advanced Git evidence block headed "# Evidence: gita-production-repository-defense-wtjhxg" with exact before and after refs; a falsifiable prediction; the bounded simulator command; expected, observed, tree, behavior, changed-case, and recovery evidence; and one honest real-repository transfer limit.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Gita Production Repository Defense into observable performance evidence.

content.code:
# Evidence: gita-production-repository-defense-wtjhxg
# scenario-key: trace-wtjhxg probe-gxhjtw boundary-jhxgwt owner-wtjhxgx fault-wtjhxgz
# before-ref: main=a11cewt candidate=b00b1xg
# hypothesis: the repository stores SHA-256 objects while one integration still assumes 40-character IDs
git rev-parse --show-object-format=storage,input,output
# expected: format evidence separates storage identity from accepted input and displayed output formats
# observation: storage reports sha256 and the audit parser fails its fixed-width object-ID contract
# changed-case: restore a bundle into a fresh same-format repository and reject an incompatible toolchain
# after-ref: main=a11cewt candidate=c0ffeeg
# tree-proof: the affected tree and path set match the predicted repository transition
# behavior-proof: a changed input preserves the named product or release invariant
# recovery: preserve trusted refs, object stores, translation data, manifests, and exact loss accounting
# transfer-limit: object-format migration and Git 3.0 rollout require current hosting and tool compatibility trials
content.caption: Shape of the required increment; replace generic language with the current stakeholder case.

hints: Name the Gita Production Repository Defense precondition or invariant that the lab case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: Case G36 is a lab in a disposable repository. the repository stores SHA-256 objects while one integration still assumes 40-character IDs. The learner must predict format evidence separates storage identity from accepted input and displayed output formats, run git rev-parse --show-object-format=storage,input,output, explain storage reports sha256 and the audit parser fails its fixed-width object-ID contract, test this changed condition: restore a bundle into a fresh same-format repository and reject an incompatible toolchain, and preserve this recovery boundary: preserve trusted refs, object stores, translation data, manifests, and exact loss accounting. Competency probe: Defend repository policy, topology, recovery, scale, trust, release identity, maintenance, migration, drills, residual risks, and owner handoff under changed constraints.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: A successful restore drill proves future incidents will have identical data, access, timing, and failure modes.

stimulus.title: Gita Production Repository Defense failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: A successful restore drill proves future incidents will have identical data, access, timing, and failure modes.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Defend repository policy, topology, recovery, scale, trust, release identity, maintenance, migration, drills, residual risks, and owner handoff under changed constraints.

options.text: Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Gita Production Repository Defense condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.

Keep the plausible Gita Production Repository Defense output and hide the violated Git 3.0 readiness and disaster-recovery defense assumption instead of explaining the failure. This does not resolve test an acceptance boundary.

For test an acceptance boundary, apply Gita Production Repository Defense without its stated preconditions, omit the Git 3.0 readiness and disaster-recovery defense trace and counterexample, and treat one plausible output as complete proof. This does not satisfy Gita Production Repository Defense for Git 3.0 readiness and disaster-recovery defense.

steps.title: Defend the transferred model
steps.instruction: Write the lab evidence note for Gita Production Repository Defense: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — Case G37 is a lab in a disposable repository. the repository stores SHA-256 objects while one integration still assumes 40-character IDs. The learner must predict format evidence separates storage identity from accepted input and displayed output formats, run git rev-parse --show-object-format=storage,input,output, explain storage reports sha256 and the audit parser fails its fixed-width object-ID contract, test this changed condition: restore a bundle into a fresh same-format repository and reject an incompatible toolchain, and preserve this recovery boundary: preserve trusted refs, object stores, translation data, manifests, and exact loss accounting. Competency probe: Defend repository policy, topology, recovery, scale, trust, release identity, maintenance, migration, drills, residual risks, and owner handoff under changed constraints.
steps.why: Retrieving Gita Production Repository Defense through the concrete Git 3.0 readiness and disaster-recovery defense case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Defend repository policy, topology, recovery, scale, trust, release identity, maintenance, migration, drills, residual risks, and owner handoff under changed constraints.
